edition: Situational Judgement Full Mock · Original v1
sectionLabelZh: 情境判断完整模考 · 满托原创
rightsNotice: 本模考依据 UCAT 公开分测验结构与职业素养范围独立命题，不复制或改写官方 Question Bank、Practice Test、情境、选项或解析。
scoringNotice: 等级题完全匹配得 1 分，相邻等级得 0.5 分；最合适/最不合适题需两项均匹配得 1 分。本站只报告满托原创卷原始分，不生成官方 SJT Band。

sourceAnchors.usedFor: current Situational Judgement identity, 69-question count, 26-minute timing and partial-credit boundary only
appropriateness, importance and most/least interaction families only
professional-topic coverage audit only; it is not an official UCAT answer key

scenarios.title: A mismatched label
scenarios.context: During a supervised placement, Maya notices that the name on a sample label does not match the name on the request form. The sample has not yet been processed, and the supervising clinician is nearby.

items.prompt: Pause the task and tell the supervising clinician exactly what does not match.
Change the label to the name Maya remembers hearing earlier.
Ask another student whether the mismatch is probably harmless, while allowing the sample to continue.
Make a factual note of what was observed after alerting the supervisor.
Wait until the end of the placement week before mentioning the mismatch.

scenarios.title: A corridor conversation
scenarios.context: Leo hears a fellow student discussing an identifiable patient's condition in a busy corridor where visitors can hear. The conversation is not needed for the patient's care.

items.prompt: Quietly interrupt and suggest continuing any necessary discussion in a private place.
Join the conversation and add another detail because the information is already being discussed.
Walk away and take no action because Leo did not start the conversation.
Afterwards, speak privately to the student about confidentiality and seek guidance if the behaviour continues.
Post online that students at the placement have poor confidentiality, without naming the patient.

scenarios.title: Repeatedly missed group tasks
scenarios.context: Aisha is coordinating a student health-promotion project. One member has missed two agreed tasks and has recently appeared distracted. The final presentation is next week.

items.prompt: Speak privately with the student, describe the missed tasks, ask whether support is needed, and agree a realistic plan.
Remove the student's name from the presentation without speaking to them.
Complete all the missing work without telling anyone and hope the pattern stops.
Agree clear responsibilities and a check-in point for the whole group.
Seek advice from the project tutor if the agreed plan fails or the student's wellbeing appears at risk.

scenarios.title: A photograph for revision
scenarios.context: During a dental placement, Sam wants to photograph a treatment setup for revision. No patient is visible, but local policy and consent arrangements are not known.

items.prompt: Ask the supervisor and follow the placement's policy before taking any photograph.
Take the photograph now and check whether it was allowed later.
Take the photograph on a personal phone because no patient can be seen.
Use an approved teaching image already provided by the placement instead.
Ask another student whether photographs are usually tolerated and rely on their answer.

scenarios.title: Unwell before placement
scenarios.context: Nina wakes with fever and dizziness before a patient-facing placement. Attendance is assessed, and she is worried that reporting sick will affect her record.

items.prompt: Contact the placement promptly, explain the symptoms honestly, and follow health and absence guidance.
Attend and avoid mentioning the symptoms unless someone notices.
Ask a friend to record Nina as present.
Seek appropriate health advice rather than relying only on Nina's own judgement.
Tell the placement only after the session has finished.

scenarios.title: A question beyond the student's role
scenarios.context: A patient asks Omar, a student, whether a recent test means that the patient has a serious illness. Omar has seen the result but has not been asked to interpret it, and the clinician responsible is available.

items.prompt: Explain that Omar is a student and arrange for the responsible clinician to discuss the result.
Offer a confident interpretation so that the patient is not left waiting.
Listen to the patient's concern and pass it promptly to the responsible clinician.
Say that students are not allowed to speak with patients and leave immediately.
Ask another student to interpret the result instead.

scenarios.title: A patient raises a concern
scenarios.context: A patient tells Priya, a student, that a member of the team repeatedly dismissed their questions. Priya did not witness the conversations and needs to decide what to do next.

items.prompt: Whether the patient may currently be at risk or unable to make an informed decision.
The specific facts the patient can describe, separated from assumptions.
Whether Priya personally likes the member of staff involved.
The patient's perspective and what outcome they are seeking.
The appropriate route for sharing the concern with a supervisor.

scenarios.title: An incomplete handover
scenarios.context: At the end of a placement session, Daniel realises that information he was asked to pass to the next student team was incomplete. A supervisor and the next team are still contactable.

items.prompt: Whether the missing information could affect immediate care or safety.
How to communicate the corrected information accurately and promptly.
Whether admitting the omission might be embarrassing for Daniel.
Which supervisor can help verify and pass on the information.
Who originally caused the information to be incomplete.

scenarios.title: A thank-you gift
scenarios.context: A patient offers Elena, a student, an expensive gift after several placement visits. Elena wants to respond respectfully without creating an inappropriate personal obligation.

items.prompt: The value and nature of the gift.
The placement's policy and the supervisor's guidance.
The patient's likely intention in offering thanks.
Whether Elena personally wants the gift.
Whether accepting it could affect trust or professional boundaries.

scenarios.title: A discriminatory comment
scenarios.context: During a teaching session, a student makes a discriminatory remark about a patient group. Another student appears upset, and the tutor does not seem to have heard it.

items.prompt: The possible impact on the student who heard the remark and on the learning environment.
Whether the remark reflects a risk to fair and respectful patient care.
How popular the student who made the remark is.
What support and reporting routes are available.
Whether this is part of a repeated pattern.

scenarios.title: A task beyond current competence
scenarios.context: A busy clinician asks Hugo, a student, to carry out a patient-facing task that Hugo has observed but never performed. The clinician begins to leave the room.

items.prompt: Hugo's actual competence and experience with the task.
Whether suitable supervision will remain available.
The patient's safety, understanding and agreement to student involvement.
Whether declining will make Hugo look less confident.
The potential learning value if the task can be made safe.

scenarios.title: An unexpected research result
scenarios.context: Mei is helping with a student research project. One result is inconsistent with the source record, and correcting it may weaken the group's conclusion shortly before submission.

items.prompt: The need for the dataset and report to reflect the source accurately.
Whether the difference could have arisen from an honest data-entry error.
The correct route for checking the record and informing the supervisor.
How disappointed the group may be if the conclusion changes.
Whether the person who entered the data is Mei's friend.

scenarios.title: A distressed teammate
scenarios.context: Just before a patient-education session, Ben notices that his teammate is visibly distressed and says they cannot concentrate.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for Ben.
actions: Speak privately, check immediate safety, and involve the supervisor so the session can be covered safely.
Tell the teammate to hide how they feel because the session matters more.
Announce to the waiting group that the teammate is emotionally unstable.

scenarios.title: A misdirected email
scenarios.context: Fatima realises that she sent identifiable placement information to the wrong university email address moments ago.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for Fatima.
actions: Report the error promptly through the approved supervisor or information-governance route and follow instructions.
Delete the message from her sent folder and say nothing unless someone complains.
Ask the unintended recipient not to open or share it, while also reporting the incident.

scenarios.title: Conflict in a project group
scenarios.context: Two members of Sofia's project group accuse each other of withholding work. The deadline is approaching, but neither has shown Sofia the relevant messages or files.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for Sofia.
actions: Hear each person separately, establish the facts, and agree transparent responsibilities and a review point.
Remove the quieter member from the project because they are less likely to object.
Send both accusations to the tutor immediately without first clarifying what happened.

scenarios.title: A request for personal contact
scenarios.context: A patient asks Jack, a student, to connect on a personal social-media account so they can continue discussing health questions after the placement.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for Jack.
actions: Explain the professional boundary politely and direct the patient to an appropriate clinical contact route.
Accept the request but promise not to discuss anything confidential.
Walk away without answering or helping the patient find an appropriate route.

scenarios.title: A disrespectful remark
scenarios.context: During a break, Arun hears a student make a demeaning remark about another student's accent. The targeted student becomes silent.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for Arun.
actions: Challenge the remark calmly, check on the targeted student, and seek support if needed.
Laugh along so the group does not become uncomfortable.
Check privately on the targeted student and later ask a tutor how best to address the remark.

scenarios.title: A missed deadline
scenarios.context: Grace forgets to submit a required reflective log. The online deadline passed that morning, and the tutor has not contacted her.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for Grace.
actions: Contact the tutor promptly, explain honestly, submit the work, and ask how to remedy the missed deadline.
Alter the file metadata and claim the system failed before the deadline.
Upload the work late without any explanation and hope it is not noticed.

scenarios.title: A language barrier
scenarios.context: A patient is struggling to understand information during a student-observed consultation. An adult relative offers to translate, but sensitive matters may be discussed and an approved interpreting service is available.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for the student to suggest.
actions: Ask the clinician to use the approved interpreting service and check the patient's communication preferences.
Use a young family member who has arrived, because this would be faster.
Use clear language and confirm understanding while the approved support is arranged.

scenarios.title: A safety step is missed
scenarios.context: A student notices a peer about to begin patient contact after missing a routine safety step. The peer can correct it immediately and no harm has occurred.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for the observing student.
actions: Prompt the peer discreetly and immediately so the step is completed before contact.
Post a critical message about the peer in the student group chat after the session.
Say nothing because correcting peers is only a supervisor's responsibility.

scenarios.title: A shared revision file
scenarios.context: Lina opens a shared student revision file and finds a detailed account of a real placement encounter that could identify the patient.
scenarios.prompt: Select the most appropriate and least appropriate action for Lina.
actions: Stop sharing the material, alert the owner and an appropriate supervisor, and follow guidance on removing the identifiable content.
Forward the file to another group because it is useful for revision.
Ask the owner privately to anonymise the account but keep using the current version meanwhile.
